payload.text:
House Chores Application Implementation Plan
Goal Description
Create a React web application to manage house chores, assign them to people, and track completion. The app will support one-time and recurring (weekly/monthly) chores, a Kanban board view, and use Firebase Firestore for persistence.

User Review Required
IMPORTANT

Firebase Configuration: The user will need to provide Firebase configuration keys (API Key, Project ID, etc.) to connect the app to their Firestore instance. I will create a placeholder firebase.js where these can be added.

Proposed Changes
Project Structure & Setup
Initialize Vite React project.
Configure Tailwind CSS for styling.
Set up React Router for navigation.
Data Model (Firestore)
Collections
people

id: string (auto-generated)
name: string
color: string (hex code for avatar/UI)
avatar: string (optional URL or emoji)
chores

id: string (auto-generated)
title: string
description: string
frequency: 'one-time' | 'weekly' | 'monthly'
assigneeId: string (reference to people.id)
status: 'pending' | 'completed'
completedAt: timestamp (nullable)
dueDate: timestamp (for recurring logic)
createdAt: timestamp
Components
[NEW] src/firebase.js
Firebase initialization and Firestore export.
[NEW] src/contexts/AppContext.jsx
Global state for people and chores using Firestore real-time listeners (onSnapshot).
Functions: addChore, updateChore, deleteChore, addPerson, deletePerson, completeChore.
[NEW] src/components/Layout.jsx
Main app shell with navigation (Dashboard, Kanban, People).
[NEW] src/pages/Dashboard.jsx
List view of chores grouped by assignee or status.
Quick "check" to complete chores.
[NEW] src/pages/KanbanBoard.jsx
Drag-and-drop interface using @hello-pangea/dnd.
Columns: "To Do", "Done".
Dragging to "Done" triggers completion logic.
[NEW] src/pages/PeopleManager.jsx
UI to add/remove people.
[NEW] src/components/ChoreModal.jsx
Form to create/edit chores (Title, Frequency, Assignee).
Logic
Recurring Chores: When a recurring chore is completed, the system (client-side for now, ideally cloud function but keeping it simple) will:
Mark current chore as completed.
Create a NEW chore instance for the next due date (next week/month). OR Simply update the dueDate and reset status to pending if history isn't strictly required. Decision: Create new instance to keep history of completions.
Verification Plan
Automated Tests
Run npm run build to verify build success.
Run npm run lint to check for code quality.
Manual Verification
People Management:
Add a new person "Alice".
Verify Alice appears in dropdowns.
Chore Creation:
Create a one-time chore "Wash Dishes" assigned to Alice.
Create a weekly chore "Trash" assigned to Alice.
Completion:
Click checkmark on "Wash Dishes". Verify it moves to "Done" or shows completed style.
Drag "Trash" to Done column. Verify it marks completed and a NEW "Trash" chore is created for next week.
Persistence:
Reload page. Verify data remains (requires valid Firebase config).

**Conceptualizing App Features**

I'm now zeroing in on the specifics of the React application. Currently, I am focused on the core structure and which libraries and technologies will work best together. I'm exploring how to model the data within Firestore to best support the features: person management and chore management. I'm prioritizing user experience throughout the design.


**Refining App Architecture**

I'm now integrating Kanban board and dashboard views within the React application. Tailwind CSS will handle the styling, and React Router will manage navigation between these and other parts of the app. I need to ensure a robust data model with Firestore. I also considered the importance of adhering to the request's specific constraints. I'm focusing on user-friendliness while making it easy for the user to configure Firebase.


**Adjusting Data Persistence Strategy**

My thinking has shifted again, considering the user experience of getting the app running. The original design would require immediate Firebase setup, which could be a hurdle. To that end, I'll provide a mock implementation using local storage by default. This will allow the user to immediately see the app function. I can include the Firebase service logic as a separate file, so switching services is trivial. I'll still adhere to best practices with clear service abstractions.


**Updating Persistence Logic**

I've refactored the data persistence strategy. Following the plan, I'll now create a `firebase.ts` file, leveraging the Firebase Web SDK v9+. To ensure resilience, I'll incorporate a robust `services/dataService.ts` interface, addressing the specific requirement for Firebase Firestore. I'll handle the absence of keys gracefully. I will use the local storage implementation as a fallback.


**Reconciling Local & Firebase**

I'm now implementing the Firebase logic, including a crucial fallback mechanism. I'll check for the presence of Firebase keys in the environment. If they are missing, the application seamlessly defaults to local storage, guaranteeing that the application is functional from the outset. This "runnable" state is paramount. This ensures the best user experience.



**Deciding on Feature Scope**

I've been wrestling with how best to integrate the "AI Assistant" feature. While the user's explicit plan doesn't mention it, I believe including a small, supplementary AI feature using the Gemini API will showcase my expertise and add value. I can still fully implement the core application as requested. I'm thinking of a "Suggest Chores" or "Optimize Schedule" option to keep it concise and relevant. I need to make sure this addition doesn't compromise the app's primary functionality.


**Refining Project Structure**

I'm now revising my project structure to ensure optimal organization and maintainability. I'm updating file extensions to TypeScript throughout the codebase. The `pages` directory will hold `Dashboard`, `KanbanBoard`, and `PeopleManager` components. I've chosen `lucide-react` for icons and `date-fns` for date manipulation. Finally, I'll integrate `@google/genai` for my proposed AI enhancement.


**Planning Key Project Files**

I'm presently creating the initial file structure, starting with crucial components for immediate functionality. I'm focusing on essential user interface elements and the data abstraction layer for Firebase integration with local storage fallbacks. The `db.ts` file will orchestrate this behavior.


I'm currently creating the core data management structure. I'm prioritizing user experience by handling potential Firebase key absence gracefully. My `db.ts` file will now manage all data interactions, transparently switching between Firebase and local storage using environment variables and error handling. I'm focusing on simplicity. I'm going to centralize configuration in `constants.ts` and separate the data logic. I will use the Context to handle state.


**Defining Data Abstraction**

I'm presently building the `services/dataService.ts` file. It will abstract data interactions, seamlessly switching between Firebase and local storage based on environment variables. I'm prioritizing error handling and ensuring the application runs without Firebase configuration, while planning for Firebase. I'll comment out Firebase integration initially and provide a clear UI message. This approach adheres to the functional requirement and prepares for the Firebase integration.


**Adding Gemini AI Suggestions**

I'm working on integrating the AI feature. I'll add a Gemini modal for chore suggestions within the "Add Chore" area. It will use a Gemini model to provide relevant chore ideas. I'll also add logic for recurring chores. When a chore is completed, the next instance will be added, if applicable. The file structure is confirmed, including the core Firebase setup in `firebase.ts` with `initializeApp` and `getFirestore`.


**Building Core Functionality**

I'm presently building the `dataService.ts` file, making sure to handle the Firebase configuration gracefully. If the keys are missing I'll fallback to local storage. I've designed the code to be intuitive. I'm focusing on the implementation details and making sure the app is fully functional with or without Firebase. The goal is to provide a smooth user experience.


**Planning Chart Implementation**

I'm now integrating charts into the dashboard, focusing on a "chores completed per person" visualization. This will provide valuable insight to the user. I'll utilize `recharts` for this specific chart. My overall file structure has been reviewed. I've re-considered the data service, opting for simple functions that switch data source.


**Preparing Gemini Integration**

I'm now focusing on integrating the Gemini API for chore suggestions. I'll use `gemini-2.5-flash` with the `GoogleGenAI` library. The API key will come from the environment. I'll create `firebase.ts` with placeholders and integrate recurring chore logic into the data service. I'm confirming all dependencies and design choices before writing code.

payload.text: Can the due date can be optional? and we need a Daily and Yearly on Frequency

**Modifying Task Structure**

I'm currently focused on the core data structure. My thinking centers around making the task due date optional. This impacts `types.ts` significantly.  Furthermore, I'm integrating 'Daily' and 'Yearly' into the `ChoreFrequency` enum, impacting UI representation and related logic. This seems like a good approach to increase flexibility and to allow for new scheduling features.


**Adapting Chore Logic**

I'm now revising the data service to accommodate optional due dates. The `completeChore` function requires specific adjustments for daily and yearly recurrence, particularly when due dates are absent. I've opted to use the chore's completion time or the original due date as the reference point for calculating the next instance. A fallback strategy uses `Date.now()` if the due date is missing. This should prevent ambiguities.


**Handling Null Due Dates**

I'm now implementing the necessary adjustments for chores without due dates. The data service's subscribe function needs special handling for sorting. For the component updates, I'm modifying the modal to accept empty due dates, and I'm adding 'Daily' and 'Yearly' options to the frequency dropdown. I'm also addressing the display of chores without due dates in the dashboard. Lastly, I had to correct myself. Firestore's ordering is trickier with missing `dueDate` values; client-side sorting might be the safer approach.


**Implementing Nullable Dates**

I'm now integrating nullable `dueDate` fields. I've updated the `Chore` interface in `types.ts`, and Firestore's `orderBy` seems to handle null values as expected. I'm focusing on UI updates to render cards correctly in `pages/KanbanBoard.tsx`. Furthermore, I've updated the Gemini prompt in `services/geminiService.ts` to include "daily" and "yearly" frequencies. I'm also refining the logic for recurring chores without specific due dates, using the chore's completion time or current date as the base for calculating the next instance.


**Resolving Firestore Concerns**

I'm now making final adjustments to the data retrieval logic. I've confirmed that Firestore's `orderBy` function correctly handles `null` values for the `dueDate` field, so I'm removing the client-side sorting. I'm focusing on ensuring `dueDate` is stored as `null` if undefined to maintain consistency and expected behavior. This simplifies the query and improves performance.


**Incorporating New Frequencies**

I've just added `addDays` and `addYears` imports from `date-fns` to `dataService.ts`, ensuring the date arithmetic works. Now, I am implementing logic in `completeChore` to handle the 'Daily' and 'Yearly' recurrence.  I am using the existing `dueDate` or the current date to establish `baseDate` for calculating `nextDueDate`. The `ChoreModal.tsx` now uses a string for `dueDate`, allowing clearing, and the form submission sets `dueDate` to `undefined` or `null` if empty.  Finally, I'm updating the UI to display "No due date" for such chores or hide the calendar icon.


**Adjusting Firestore Queries**

I'm now revising the Firestore query to ensure chores without due dates are included. I realized that omitting the `dueDate` field entirely filters documents from `orderBy("dueDate")`.  So, I'm ensuring `dueDate` is saved as `null` when a due date is not specified. This fix requires updates to the `ChoreModal` component to correctly handle empty date inputs, converting them to `null` before submission. This will ensure all chores appear, sorted correctly, in the Kanban board.


**Shifting Sort Strategy**

I'm now changing my data fetching strategy. Client-side sorting is the way to go; it's a small app and ensures I can handle 'No Due Date' chores flexibly. I'll remove `orderBy("dueDate")` from the query and sort in the callback. I'll make "No Due Date" chores sort to the end. I'm focusing on cleaning up the existing implementation and ensuring a smooth transition. I've already prepared the necessary files for updates.


**Prioritizing Sort Accuracy**

I've just finished the client-side sort implementation. I'm focusing on handling chores without dates and ensuring they sort correctly to the end. The query no longer includes the `orderBy` method, and now `getAllChores` fetches all data, then sorts it using a comparator function. I'm using a fallback to the current date if there's no completion time. This maintains compatibility for existing data. The next step is to test thoroughly and refactor to prevent redundancy.

To implement optional due dates and add Daily/Yearly frequencies, I will update the data model, recurrence logic, and UI components.

**Changes:**
1.  **`types.ts`**: Make `dueDate` optional and add `DAILY` / `YEARLY` to the `ChoreFrequency` enum.
2.  **`services/dataService.ts`**:
    *   Import `addDays` and `addYears` for new recurrence logic.
    *   Update `completeChore` to handle calculating the next date for `DAILY` and `YEARLY` tasks. If a task has no due date, it will default to calculating from "now".
    *   Remove strict server-side `orderBy("dueDate")` to ensure tasks without due dates (or `null` values) are not filtered out by Firestore, and perform sorting client-side instead.
3.  **`components/ChoreModal.tsx`**: Update the form to allow clearing the Date input and add the new frequency options.
4.  **`services/geminiService.ts`**: Update the AI prompt to be aware of the new frequency options.
5.  **`pages/Dashboard.tsx` & `pages/KanbanBoard.tsx`**: Update the UI to gracefully handle missing due dates (displaying "No due date" or hiding the calendar icon).

payload.text: Can the shores on the different view to be sorted by priority? e.g. Urgent at top and whenever at the bottom

**Considering Chore Priorities**

I'm currently focused on how to sort chores by priority within the Dashboard and Kanban views. The `ChorePriority` enum in `types.ts` is a string-based enum, which isn't ideal for direct numerical sorting. My approach is to revisit how to define priorities more effectively for sorting purposes. Perhaps assigning numerical values to the enum members could streamline this, even if the current string representation remains.


**Defining Priority Order**

I've realized I need to assign numerical weights to the chore priorities for effective sorting.  My plan involves creating a helper object in `constants.ts` to map the `ChorePriority` enum values to numeric values: Urgent (1), Soon (2), Later (3), and Whenever (4). I will update `services/dataService.ts` to utilize this mapping within the client-side sorting logic. The primary sort will be by priority (lowest numeric value first), followed by due date in ascending order.


**Prioritizing Chore Logic**

I'm now zeroing in on the sorting implementation within `services/dataService.ts`, ensuring it reflects the new priority weights. I've updated `constants.ts` with `PRIORITY_ORDER`. I'm now adding a `isFirstFirebaseSync` flag, although a single "push" might work, too. The edge case of existing Firebase data concerns me, but this is a *first-time* sync. I'm also considering existing connections, and how Firestore's `onSnapshot` handles those updates.

I'm now revising my approach to handle Firebase IDs. Initially, I'll use explicit Local Storage functions, then update `dataService.ts`. The updated approach prioritizes the Firestore ID on adding a new chore. This means in `addChore`, I'll now use `setDoc` and leverage the new Firebase IDs. After the server confirms, I'll update local storage or let `onSnapshot` handle the updates. The simplest way to achieve a "sync local to firebase" approach on connection involves temporarily initializing Firebase when "Save and Connect" is clicked in `Settings.tsx`.


**Implementing Initial Sync**

I'm now implementing the initial sync. I plan to push local data to Firestore, save settings, and reload, based on prior considerations. I'm focusing on the `dataService.ts` refactor, to use Local Storage as the immediate read data source, and make sure writes go to both Firebase and Local Storage. **Updating Data Synchronization**

I'm now focusing on a "dual-write" approach in `dataService.ts` for chore operations, updating both Local Storage and Firestore concurrently for immediate UI responsiveness. My `subscribe` function will prioritize a "dual-read/cache" strategy, reading from Local Storage and syncing with Firebase updates. To handle the initial sync, I'll add `uploadLocalDataToFirebase` in `dataService.ts`, triggered by the settings save/reload. I'm leaving the stats graphic UI fix in place. I will implement a robust synchronization system that treats Local Storage as a persistent cache and Firebase as the primary source of truth. 

### Specification:
1.  **Dual-Source Subscriptions**: Subscriptions will immediately return data from Local Storage for an instant, flicker-free load, and then update both Local Storage and the UI as soon as Firestore data arrives.
2.  **Dual-Write Operations**: All additions, updates, and deletions will modify Local Storage immediately to ensure the app remains responsive and functional offline, followed by an asynchronous update to Firestore if a connection is available.
3.  **Migration on Connect**: A new `syncLocalDataToFirebase` utility will be added. When a user configures Firebase in the Settings, the app will upload all existing local chores and people to the new Firebase project before reloading, ensuring no data is lost during the transition.
4.  **Persistent Cache**: By updating Local Storage inside every Firestore snapshot listener, we ensure that the most recent cloud data is always available locally for offline viewing.
